items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: Cathode product when electrolysing copper sulfate solution?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cu. “O2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Brine electrolysis produces all of these EXCEPT:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: oxygen. “chlorine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mass of Cu deposited (Ar 63.5) from 0.2 mol electrons in Cu^2+ + 2e- -> Cu?
items.why: The real figure is 6.35. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
